## 🤖 BrainEdge

I read the repo and your `claude-ui` workspace. You've already built **CClaude** — Electron + React + Vite, a chat UI pointed at the free-claude-code proxy, with Chat, Projects, Model Selector, Settings working and Cowork/Skills/Dispatch as placeholders. So you're not starting cold; you're asking how to fill in the placeholders. Good. Here's my honest read.

## The hard part first: "any LLM" is in direct tension with "all of Claude's functionality"

The strongest objection to your spec, and you need to hear it before you write more code: **the features you're listing are mostly properties of the model, not the harness.** free-claude-code is a *translation proxy* — it makes a weak model *speak* the Anthropic wire format. It does not make a weak model *behave* like Claude. The dead giveaway is in that README: it ships a "heuristic tool parser" because the backing models "output tool calls as text" instead of structured tool-use. That's a euphemism for "the agent loop is fragile." Cowork, Claude Code, Skills, and Connectors are all just *agentic tool-calling in a loop* — and that loop is exactly what cheap models break on: malformed tool JSON, stalls, hallucinated file contents, ignored skills, choking on Claude Code's enormous system prompt. So you can absolutely build the UI and route to anything. But "all of Claude's functionality, on any LLM, working *well*" is not on the menu. It works *well* on Claude, *acceptably* on the top open models (DeepSeek-V3/R1, Kimi K2, GLM-4.6, Qwen3-Coder), and *badly* on the free-tier junk. Confidence: **high.**

## The simplification: it's one engine, not six features

Here's the insight that saves you months. **Chat, Cowork, Claude Code, Projects, Skills, and Connectors are not six subsystems. They are one agent engine with different config presets.** Don't build them separately.

That engine already exists and you don't have to write it: the **Claude Agent SDK** (TypeScript — `@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk`, formerly the Claude Code SDK). It *is* the same engine that powers Claude Code and the Cowork mode you're talking to right now. It already implements the agent loop, tool use, the filesystem/bash tools, MCP connectors, Skills loading, subagents, hooks, and session/permission management. And critically: **it speaks the Anthropic Messages API and honors `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL` / `ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN`** — the exact two env vars free-claude-code hijacks. So it drops onto your proxy with zero modification.

That collapses your whole architecture to three layers:

```
Electron UI (React — you have this)
        │  IPC / local WebSocket
Agent engine (Claude Agent SDK in Electron's main/Node process)
        │  Anthropic wire format, ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL → proxy
free-claude-code / LiteLLM proxy  →  NIM · OpenRouter · DeepSeek · local
```

Your six "features" become presets over one `query()` session:

| Your feature | What it actually is | Implementation |
|---|---|---|
| Chat | Agent with tools disabled | SDK session, `allowedTools: []`, system prompt only |
| Claude Code | Agent with file + bash tools, scoped to a repo | SDK session, working dir = repo, full tool set |
| Cowork | Agent with file tools + skills + connectors over a folder | Same session + skills dir + MCP servers, looser permissions |
| Projects | Persisted folder + conversation history | Your DB/JSON; SDK `resume`/session IDs. Pure UI/storage, no model work |
| Skills | SKILL.md folders the engine auto-loads | SDK setting/MCP `--skills`; just point at a directory |
| Connectors | MCP servers | SDK `mcpServers` config — same MCP standard, no custom work |

So the only *new* code you write is UI panels and a thin Node bridge that starts an Agent SDK session with the right preset and streams events back over IPC to your React side. Everything labeled "agentic" is handled by the SDK.

## Build choice: SDK vs. CLI-wrap

Two valid paths, and I'd pick based on how much you want to own.

**Wrap the `claude` CLI** (`claude -p --output-format stream-json --input-format stream-json`), spawned from Electron's main process with the proxy env vars set. This is exactly what siteboon/claudecodeui and the free-claude-code Discord bot do. Fastest to ship — you parse a JSON event stream and render it. Downside: you're screen-scraping a CLI contract and lose typed control. Good for a weekend-to-working prototype.

**Use the Agent SDK directly** (`@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk`). Typed, programmatic, proper hooks/permission callbacks, cleaner streaming. ~A day more setup, much better foundation. **This is what I'd recommend** given you already have an Electron/Node main process — you're not bolting on a runtime, you're importing a library. Confidence: **high** that this is the cleaner long-term base.

Either way: **keep Electron.** Your instinct was right and it's load-bearing. Claude Code / the Agent SDK need a real filesystem and a real shell. A browser-only SPA cannot give them that without you building a remote sandbox (which is most of what *this* Cowork product is). Electron gives you local files + local shell for free — that's the entire reason Cowork/Claude Code feel like they do.

## The model reality (don't skip this)

Your free-claude-code defaults route to free NIM/OpenRouter models. For *chat*, fine. For *agentic* modes (Code/Cowork/Skills/Connectors), budget for this ranking, roughly best-to-worst among the realistic non-Anthropic options: **DeepSeek-V3/R1 ≳ Kimi K2 ≳ GLM-4.6 ≳ Qwen3-Coder ≫ the free-tier grab-bag.** Below that tier the agent loop is more frustrating than useful. Design the UI to **route per-mode**: cheap/fast model for Chat, your strongest available model for anything that calls tools. The SDK and the proxy both already support per-model mapping (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku slots) — exploit it. Confidence on the ordering: **moderate** (these models churn monthly); on the principle "agentic quality tracks model strength, not harness": **high.**

One more swap to consider: **LiteLLM** instead of (or behind) free-claude-code as the proxy. It covers 100+ providers, has Anthropic passthrough, retries, budgets, and logging — more robust than a hobby proxy if you want this to be more than a toy. free-claude-code's edge is its Claude-Code-specific request *optimizations* (it mocks Claude Code's quota/title/probe calls to save tokens). You could keep it as the front and not lose those. Confidence: **moderate.**

## Bottom line

Don't reimplement Claude's features — **mount the Claude Agent SDK as your engine, point its `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL` at your proxy, and expose presets over it as UI modes.** That's the dramatically simpler approach you asked for: you've already got the shell (CClaude), and ~90% of the "hard" functionality is one SDK dependency plus a Node↔React bridge. The remaining risk is entirely model quality, which no amount of UI fixes.

Two decisions drive the next step, and I'd like your call before I scaffold anything:

1. **Engine:** Agent SDK (recommended) or CLI-wrap (faster prototype)?
2. **Target model tier:** truly-free junk (accept degraded agentic modes), or cheap-but-capable like DeepSeek/Kimi (real agentic behavior, ~pennies)?

Tell me those two and I'll draft the concrete module plan — the Node bridge, the preset configs, and which CClaude placeholders to wire first. I can also drop this whole proposal into `claude-ui` as an `ARCHITECTURE.md` if you want it in the repo. Want me to?

## 🤖 BrainEdge

"Mount the Agent SDK as your engine" means: instead of treating Claude Code as a CLI you shell out to and screen-scrape, you `import` it as a **Node library** into your Electron main process and drive it through one typed, streaming function call. The SDK *is* the same agent runtime that powers Claude Code — it even ships the native `claude` binary as a bundled dependency and spawns it for you — but it hands you a programmatic interface instead of a terminal. Your React UI becomes a front-end over that runtime. Here's the whole thing concretely.

## What the SDK actually is

`npm install @anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk`. It bundles a platform-specific Claude Code binary (on Windows that's `@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk-win32-x64/claude.exe`), so you don't install Claude Code separately. Your code calls one primary function, `query()`, which returns an async generator. You `for await` over it and get a stream of typed events — assistant text deltas, tool-use requests, tool results, permission events, a final result. That stream *is* the agent loop: model thinks → calls a tool → SDK executes it (reads a file, runs bash, calls an MCP server) → feeds the result back → repeats until done. You render those events; you don't orchestrate the loop. Confidence: **high** (verified against current docs, package `@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk`).

## Where it sits in your Electron app

```
React renderer (CClaude UI)
   │   ipcRenderer  ⇄  ipcMain         ← your bridge
Electron main process  ──────────────  ← THIS is where you "mount" the SDK
   │   import { query } from "@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk"
   │   spawns bundled claude.exe, sets ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL on it
free-claude-code / LiteLLM proxy  →  any provider
```

The SDK needs a real Node process with filesystem + shell — that's why it lives in Electron's **main** process, not the renderer. The renderer just sends prompts and renders the event stream over IPC. That single boundary is your entire backend.

## The one primitive: `query()`

A minimal main-process handler looks like this:

```js
import { query } from "@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk";

async function runTurn(prompt, opts, send) {        // send = stream to renderer via IPC
  const q = query({
    prompt,
    options: {
      cwd: opts.projectPath,                         // the folder this session operates on
      model: "sonnet",                               // alias → proxy maps it to your real model
      env: {                                         // THIS is the "any LLM" hook
        ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL: "http://localhost:8082",
        ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN: "freecc",
      },
      ...opts.preset,                                // mode-specific options (see below)
      canUseTool: async (tool, input) => {           // turns CLI prompts into UI dialogs
        const decision = await send.askPermission(tool, input);
        return decision === "allow"
          ? { behavior: "allow", updatedInput: input }
          : { behavior: "deny", message: "User declined" };
      },
    },
  });

  for await (const msg of q) {                        // the agent loop, as events
    send.event(msg);                                  // assistant text, tool_use, tool_result, result…
  }
}
```

That's the core. Everything else is configuration of `options` and rendering of `msg`.

## The "any LLM" part lives in `env`

The SDK spawns the Claude binary, which honors `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL` and `ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN` — the exact two variables free-claude-code intercepts. You set them per-session via `options.env`. So your model router becomes a UI concern: pick a provider/model in CClaude → set the proxy env (and `model` alias) for that session. The SDK never knows it's not talking to Anthropic. This is why you get Skills, MCP connectors, and the agent loop "for free" on top of your proxy — the *harness* is Claude Code; only the *weights* are swapped downstream. (Restated caveat from before: the harness travels; the quality doesn't. Weak models break the tool-call loop.)

## Your six "modes" are just `options` presets

This is the payoff of mounting one engine. Each CClaude tab is the same `query()` with a different options object:

```js
// CHAT — no tools, just the model
{ allowedTools: [], settingSources: [], systemPrompt: { preset: "claude_code", append: chatPersona } }

// CLAUDE CODE — full dev tools, scoped to a repo, manual approvals
{ cwd: repoPath, settingSources: ["project"],   // loads the repo's CLAUDE.md + .claude/settings.json
  permissionMode: "default",                     // canUseTool drives your approval dialog
  additionalDirectories: [] }

// COWORK — file tools + skills + connectors over a folder, looser
{ cwd: folderPath,
  allowedTools: ["Read","Write","Edit","Bash","Glob","Grep"],
  mcpServers: { gmail: {...}, drive: {...} },     // ← Connectors
  agents: { researcher: {...} },                  // ← subagents
  settingSources: ["project"] }                   // ← Skills load from the folder's skill dirs
```

So **Connectors = the `mcpServers` option** (standard MCP, you write nothing custom — just config). **Skills = SKILL.md folders the SDK auto-discovers** when you enable `settingSources`/skill directories. **Projects = your own storage** (a folder path + a saved `sessionId`), not an SDK feature you build. You're not implementing six features; you're authoring six option objects.

## Sessions, history, and "Projects"

Each `query()` emits a `sessionId`. Persist it with the project. To continue a conversation, pass `resume: sessionId`; to branch from a past point (your "new chat from here"), add `forkSession: true`. For a live multi-turn chat where the user keeps typing, use the streaming-input form — `query()` accepts an async iterable of messages and exposes `streamInput()` / `interrupt()` so a "Stop" button actually halts the agent mid-tool. That interrupt capability is something a naive CLI-wrap struggles to do cleanly; the SDK gives it to you as a method call. Confidence: **high**.

## The part that makes it a UI instead of a CLI: `canUseTool` + `hooks`

This is the crux of your whole objective. In the terminal, Claude Code pauses and asks "Allow Bash to run `rm`? (y/n)". In your app, that same decision arrives as a **`canUseTool` callback** — an async function the SDK awaits. You forward it over IPC, render a real approval dialog in React, and resolve the promise with `{behavior:"allow"}` or `{behavior:"deny"}`. The agent blocks until the user clicks. That single callback is how you convert every interactive CLI prompt into native UI. `hooks` (events like `PreToolUse`, `PostToolUse`, `SessionStart`) give you the rest: logging, telemetry, auto-allow rules, redaction, custom side effects — all as callbacks rather than terminal output you'd otherwise parse. Confidence: **high**.

## The Electron gotchas you'll actually hit

Two real ones, both verified. First, the bundled binary: the SDK pulls a native `claude.exe` as an *optional* dependency. electron-builder must not prune it, and after packaging you'll likely need to point `pathToClaudeCodeExecutable` at the unpacked binary inside your app's resources (asar unpacking the SDK's platform package). If the optional dep gets skipped, the SDK throws `Native CLI binary for <platform> not found`. Second, the agent must run in **main**, never the renderer — keep API keys/proxy tokens out of the renderer entirely and pass only rendered events across IPC. Confidence: **high** on the binary requirement; **moderate** on the exact electron-builder unpack incantation (depends on your build config).

## Honest verdict on "mounting" vs CLI-wrap

Mounting the SDK costs you maybe a day more than spawning `claude -p --output-format stream-json` and parsing its NDJSON. What you buy: typed events instead of a scraped text contract, real `interrupt()`, the `canUseTool` permission hook (the thing that makes your UI feel native), and `hooks` for everything else. For a product you intend to grow, mount it. For a throwaway proof that free models can drive an agent at all, the CLI-wrap is fine and you already have the proxy. I'd mount it.

If you want, I'll scaffold the actual Electron main-process module — `agent-engine.cjs` with `query()`, the IPC bridge, the three mode presets, and a `canUseTool` round-trip to a React approval modal — wired to your existing CClaude shell. Say the word and which mode to wire first.

Sources: [Agent SDK TypeScript reference](https://docs.claude.com/en/api/agent-sdk/typescript), [Agent SDK MCP/connectors](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/mcp)

For *agentic* modes (Code/Cowork/Skills/Connectors), budget for this ranking, roughly best-to-worst among the realistic non-Anthropic options: **DeepSeek-V3/R1 ≳ Kimi K2 ≳ GLM-4.6 ≳ Qwen3-Coder ≫ the free-tier grab-bag.** Below that tier the agent loop is more frustrating than useful. Design the UI to **route per-mode**: cheap/fast model for Chat, your strongest available model for anything that calls tools. The SDK and the proxy both already support per-model mapping (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku slots) — exploit it. Confidence on the ordering: **moderate** (these models churn monthly); on the principle "agentic quality tracks model strength, not harness": **high.**

One more swap to consider: **LiteLLM** instead of (or behind) free-claude-code as the proxy. It covers 100+ providers, has Anthropic passthrough, retries, budgets, and logging — more robust than a hobby proxy if you want this to be more than a toy. free-claude-code's edge is its Claude-Code-specific request *optimizations* (it mocks Claude Code's quota/title/probe calls to save tokens). You could keep it as the front and not lose those. Confidence: **moderate.**

## Bottom line

Don't reimplement Claude's features — **mount the Claude Agent SDK as your engine, point its `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL` at your proxy, and expose presets over it as UI modes.** That's the dramatically simpler approach you asked for: you've already got the shell (CClaude), and ~90% of the "hard" functionality is one SDK dependency plus a Node↔React bridge. The remaining risk is entirely model quality, which no amount of UI fixes.

Two decisions drive the next step, and I'd like your call before I scaffold anything:

1. **Engine:** Agent SDK (recommended) or CLI-wrap (faster prototype)?
2. **Target model tier:** truly-free junk (accept degraded agentic modes), or cheap-but-capable like DeepSeek/Kimi (real agentic behavior, ~pennies)?

Tell me those two and I'll draft the concrete module plan — the Node bridge, the preset configs, and which CClaude placeholders to wire first. I can also drop this whole proposal into `claude-ui` as an `ARCHITECTURE.md` if you want it in the repo. Want me to?

this is the architectuer poposed previously by Sonnet, do you have better proposal ?

## 🤖 BrainEdge

Yes. Sonnet's diagram is the textbook trap, and I'll say exactly why before I redraw it.

## The core problem with Sonnet's design

It hand-builds the two hardest pieces that are already solved, and it builds them in a way that makes them fight each other. Look at three of its boxes:

- **"Provider Abstraction Layer"** with a `sendMessage()`/`listModels()` per provider — that is re-implementing LiteLLM by hand, one class at a time, forever chasing each provider's quirks. The free-claude-code repo you started from *is* that layer. You'd be rebuilding what you already have.
- **"Cowork Agent Runtime — file ops, shell, system tools"** — that is re-implementing Claude Code's tool suite *and its agent loop and its permission model* from scratch.
- **"MCP Server Manager"** — that is re-implementing MCP spawning/supervision/tool-routing.

And here's the killer: a per-provider `sendMessage()` gives you a **chat** primitive, not an **agent**. So the "Cowork Agent Runtime" box has to wrap a multi-step tool loop (tool dispatch, tool-result turns, parallel calls, streaming partial blocks, permission gating, subagents) *around each provider's bespoke format*. That's an N-providers × M-agent-features maintenance matrix. You will spend 90% of your time on plumbing that Anthropic already ships and tests. Confidence: **high.**

## The better architecture: collapse two custom boxes into one dependency

The Claude Agent SDK (`@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk`) *is* the agent runtime + MCP manager + tool suite + skills + permissions + sessions, in one package — it bundles the native Claude Code binary, so there's nothing to reimplement. Provider fan-out doesn't belong in your Node code at all; it belongs in the proxy, behind one wire format. Same ASCII style, redrawn:

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        CClaude.exe                        │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │                 React UI (Renderer)                 │  │
│  │  Chat │ Claude Code │ Cowork │ Projects │ Skills    │  │
│  │       │ Connectors  │ Settings / Model picker       │  │
│  └────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────┘  │
│                          IPC (stream SDK events)          │
│  ┌────────────────────────▼───────────────────────────┐  │
│  │              Electron Main (Node.js)                │  │
│  │  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │  │
│  │  │   Session Manager  ← the ONLY code you write   │ │  │
│  │  │   • build Options preset per mode              │ │  │
│  │  │   • query() ; pipe SDK messages → IPC          │ │  │
│  │  │   • canUseTool() → UI permission prompts       │ │  │
│  │  │   • resume / forkSession ↔ Projects + history  │ │  │
│  │  └───────────────────────┬───────────────────────┘ │  │
│  │  ┌───────────────────────▼───────────────────────┐ │  │
│  │  │   Claude Agent SDK — ALREADY BUILT:            │ │  │
│  │  │   agent loop · Read/Write/Edit/Bash/Glob/Grep  │ │  │
│  │  │   · permission model · MCP manager · Skills ·  │ │  │
│  │  │   subagents · sessions/resume                  │ │  │
│  │  └───────────────────────┬───────────────────────┘ │  │
│  └──────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────┘  │
└─────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────┘
        ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL  →  │             │ spawns
              ┌────────────────▼──┐   ┌───────▼────────┐
              │ Proxy             │   │  MCP servers   │
              │ LiteLLM / free-   │   │ GitHub·Slack·  │
              │ claude-code       │   │ FS·custom      │
              │ ONE wire format,  │   └────────────────┘
              │ N providers       │
              └────────┬──────────┘
        ┌──────────────┼────────────────┐
   Anthropic     OpenRouter/Groq/   Ollama/LM Studio
   (direct)      DeepSeek/Together   (local)
```

The model picker isn't bespoke provider classes either — it reads the proxy's `/v1/models` endpoint (free-claude-code and LiteLLM both expose it). What each of Sonnet's boxes actually costs, versus where it lives in this design:

| Sonnet's box | What building it really means | Here it becomes |
|---|---|---|
| Provider Abstraction Layer | Re-implement LiteLLM: per-provider format, streaming, tool-call mapping | **Deleted** — one Anthropic wire format; proxy fans out to N providers |
| Cowork Agent Runtime | Re-implement Claude Code's tools + agent loop + permissions | SDK built-in tools + `canUseTool` / `permissionMode` / hooks |
| MCP Server Manager | Spawn/supervise stdio+HTTP MCP, handshakes, tool routing | `mcpServers` option (or `.mcp.json` + `settingSources`) |
| Skills (not in his diagram) | — | `settingSources` + a skills dir, auto-loaded |
| The agent loop across providers | The hardest box; combinatorial with N providers | The SDK *is* the loop — built once |

Your six tabs are then just `Options` presets handed to one `query()` call: Chat = `allowedTools: []`; Claude Code = full tools, `cwd` = repo; Cowork = tools + skills + MCP, looser `permissionMode`; Projects = `resume`/`forkSession` over saved session IDs; Skills/Connectors = config, not code. Provider routing is `ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL` → proxy. The SDK passes env through to the bundled binary, which is why this works at all — it's the same mechanism free-claude-code already uses on the CLI.

## Where Sonnet's design is actually the right call (the steelman)

Don't take this as "his is always wrong." His architecture wins in three cases, and you should pick based on which you're in:

1. **You don't actually want the agent.** If the real goal is multi-provider *chat* with a few optional tools you control, the Agent SDK is heavyweight overkill and a thin provider layer is simpler and lighter. Confidence: **high.**
2. **You want native provider features** the Anthropic wire format can't express — OpenAI's responses API, Gemini-native multimodal, provider-specific params. Proxy translation is lossy; direct provider classes aren't. Confidence: **moderate.**
3. **Dependency / ToS aversion.** The SDK bundles the Claude Code binary and you'd be running Anthropic's agent harness against non-Anthropic models. That's exactly what free-claude-code does, but it's a gray area that could break or be disallowed, and it's a heavier native dependency than a few `fetch` calls. If you need a clean-room, fully-owned stack, Sonnet's hand-rolled approach has no such entanglement. Confidence: **moderate.**

Given your stated goal — Chat **plus** Cowork, Claude Code, Skills, and Connectors at feature parity — you're squarely in the case where the SDK wins, because items 4–6 on your list *are* the agent loop, and that's the one thing Sonnet's design forces you to build by hand. My one refinement to my own proposal: route the plain **Chat** tab straight at the proxy (skip the SDK) and reserve the SDK for the agentic modes. Best of both — you don't pay the heavy harness cost for simple chat.

Net: keep your Electron shell, replace Sonnet's two custom runtime boxes with one SDK dependency, push all provider variety into the proxy. Less code, more capability, and the hard parts are Anthropic's problem instead of yours.

## 🤖 BrainEdge

`ARCHITECTURE.md` is in your repo. It consolidates every good point into one design:

- **Diagram** — two transports, one event contract: plain **Chat** streams straight from the proxy (no harness tax); every agent mode goes through the SDK; provider fan-out lives in the proxy; MCP servers are SDK-spawned.
- **`SessionManager` interface** — `start / sendInput / interrupt / setPermissionMode / resolvePermission`, with the `canUseTool` → UI-permission bridge.
- **Per-mode `Options` presets** — a single table mapping Chat/Code/Cowork/Project to exact SDK fields (`systemPrompt` preset, `settingSources`, `permissionMode`, `allowedTools`, `mcpServers`, `resume`/`forkSession`).
- **SDK-event → IPC contract** — the normalized `UiEvent` envelope plus a verified mapping from each `SDKMessage` variant (`system/init`, `stream_event`, `assistant`, `user`, `result`, `SDKPermissionDeniedMessage`) and the renderer→main command channels.

Two honesty flags I baked into §6 rather than hide: confirm the `systemPrompt` preset shape and Skills settings key against your **pinned** SDK version before wiring, and keep the proxy swappable (the bundled-binary-against-non-Anthropic-models route is a gray area). Every SDK name in the doc is taken from the official TypeScript reference, not memory.

## 🧑 User

will this use Claude Desktop App ui or custom ui ?

## 🤖 BrainEdge

Custom UI — your own. Confidence: **high.**

Anthropic's Claude Desktop app is closed-source and not embeddable. There's no API or extension point to render your tabs inside it, swap its model backend, or reuse its components. You can't "skin" it or point it at your proxy. So that door is shut regardless of what you'd prefer.

What you're building is **CClaude** — the React + Vite + Electron app already in this repo. That's the custom UI. Everything in `ARCHITECTURE.md` lives there: your renderer draws the Chat/Code/Cowork/Projects/Skills/Connectors tabs, and your Electron main process runs the `SessionManager` that drives the Agent SDK. The SDK is a **headless engine** — it has no UI of its own, just `query()` and a stream of messages. You render those messages however you like.

Worth being precise about what's reused versus rebuilt, because it's easy to conflate:

- **Reused (the hard parts):** the agent *engine* — tool loop, file/shell tools, MCP, Skills, permissions, sessions. That's the Agent SDK, the same engine under Claude Code and this Cowork mode.
- **Rebuilt (yours):** the entire *visual* layer — chat bubbles, the model picker, permission modals, the tool-call/diff views, project sidebar. None of that comes from Anthropic's app; you draw it from the normalized `UiEvent` stream.

So: Claude's *brains* (the SDK), your *face* (CClaude). If your goal was specifically to get Anthropic's exact desktop look-and-feel, that's not attainable — you'd be cloning it by hand in React, and even then you can't legally lift their assets. The realistic target is a Claude-Desktop-*inspired* UI, which is exactly what your README already says CClaude is.

If you want, I can mock up the renderer layout — tab shell, message list, permission modal, model picker — as a starting React skeleton wired to the `UiEvent` contract.

New project **Chai** is scaffolded at `C:\Projects\ClaudeCodeUI\Chai` and builds clean (Vite, 1509 modules, no errors).

To see it:

```
cd C:\Projects\ClaudeCodeUI\Chai
npm run dev
```

(opens at `localhost:5174` — `npm install` already done in my sandbox, but run it once on your machine too.)

What's there, and the one idea that makes it real later: the whole UI is driven by the **`UiEvent` contract** in `src/bridge/contract.js` — the exact envelope from `ARCHITECTURE.md` §4. `App.jsx` is the reducer that turns that event stream into the timeline; it never touches a model or the SDK. `mockBridge.js` fakes the stream so the layout runs in a browser today. When you build the Electron `SessionManager`, you expose `window.chai` with the same five methods and flip the last line of `mockBridge.js` to `window.chai ?? mockBridge`. Zero UI changes.

The layout pieces you asked for: a mode/tab sidebar (Chat, Claude Code, Cowork, Projects + Skills/Connectors/Settings config panels), a topbar with a searchable **model picker** and a permission-mode chip, a streaming **message list** with collapsible **tool-call cards**, the **permission modal** (the `canUseTool` → `PermissionResult` round-trip), and a composer. Send a message in Claude Code mode to watch it stream, auto-run a `Grep`, then halt on the `Edit` permission prompt — switch to Cowork and the same edit auto-accepts.

Two honest notes: the model list and tool outputs are hardcoded demo data (real app reads the proxy's `/v1/models` and live SDK events), and this is renderer-only — no Electron main process or real proxy is wired yet. That's the next build.

**Phase 1 — Real chat, any provider (the e2e spine).** Electron shell + main process + a real `window.chai` bridge + a `SessionManager` whose **chat transport** streams from any OpenAI‑compatible *or* Anthropic‑compatible endpoint. **Phase 3 — Desktop parity + polish.** Projects persistence (resume/fork), Skills & Connectors management UIs, conversation history/search, packaging (installers), and the niceties that make it feel like the Desktop app.

## 🤖 BrainEdge

Phase 1 is built end-to-end. The three phases are in `ROADMAP.md`; here's where things stand.

**Phase 1 — real chat over any provider (done).** The full spine now exists: Electron `main.cjs` opens the window and bridges IPC; `preload.cjs` exposes the real `window.chai` implementing the same `Bridge` contract the mock used; `session-manager.cjs` runs per-session conversations and emits the normalized `UiEvent` stream; `providers.cjs` streams from either wire format (`/v1/chat/completions` for OpenAI-compatible, `/v1/messages` for Anthropic-compatible); `settings.cjs` persists provider profiles; and the new `Settings.jsx` panel lets you add/edit/activate profiles and load models. The renderer imports from `src/bridge/index.js`, which auto-selects `window.chai` in the desktop app and falls back to the mock in a plain browser — so nothing in the UI changed.

This satisfies principle 2 directly: one config schema (`kind` + `baseUrl` + `apiKey` + `model`) covers Anthropic direct, external cloud (OpenRouter/DeepSeek/Groq/Together), local (Ollama/LM Studio), and the free-claude-code proxy. Switch the active profile from the top-right picker without restarting.

**To run it on your machine:**

```
cd C:\Projects\ClaudeCodeUI\Chai
npm install
npm run electron:dev
```

Open Settings → fill a profile → Set active → start a Chat session and watch tokens stream.

**One honest caveat:** I couldn't re-run the production `vite build` in this session — the sandbox's network and file mount degraded badly (npm installs hang, the mount throws I/O errors and serves truncated reads). I verified what's verifiable without them: every `electron/*.cjs` passes `node --check`, the bridge JS is valid, the import graph is intact, and the same structure built cleanly earlier. If `npm install` on your side surfaces anything, it'll be a dependency/version issue, not a logic one — send me the error.

What's deliberately *not* in Phase 1: tools, files, MCP, skills, projects persistence. That's Phase 2 (the Agent SDK transport — the permission modal and tool-card UI are already wired and waiting for it).

Want me to start **Phase 2** — drop in the Claude Agent SDK agent transport so Claude Code and Cowork modes do real tool calls against a folder?

---

## 🤖 BrainEdge

That's not your code — it's the Electron **binary** that didn't finish downloading during `npm install`. The `electron` npm package runs a postinstall step that fetches the actual runtime from GitHub; when that step is skipped or blocked, you get exactly this `Electron failed to install correctly` error. Your `main.cjs`/`preload.cjs` never even got a chance to run. Confidence: **high.**

Fix it by reinstalling just Electron so the postinstall re-runs:

```powershell
cd C:\Projects\ClaudeCodeUI\Chai
Remove-Item -Recurse -Force node_modules\electron
npm install electron@^31.0.0 --save-dev
```

If it *still* fails (common on corporate networks/VPN/firewall blocking the GitHub release download), point it at a mirror and retry:

```powershell
$env:ELECTRON_MIRROR="https://npmmirror.com/mirrors/electron/"
Remove-Item -Recurse -Force node_modules\electron
npm install electron@^31.0.0 --save-dev
```

To see the *actual* download error if both fail, run the installer directly — it prints the real cause (proxy, 403, cert):

```powershell
node node_modules\electron\install.js
```

Two side notes from your log:

- **Port drift.** Vite said "Port 5174 is in use, trying another one" and moved to 5175, but `main.cjs` loads `localhost:5174` — a leftover Vite from the earlier crashed run is squatting 5174. Close stray terminals (or `Get-Process node | Stop-Process`) before relaunching. I'm also making the port deterministic so it fails loudly instead of silently drifting:

- **The npm warnings/audit lines are harmless** — deprecated transitive deps and audit noise from `electron-builder`'s tree. Ignore them for now; they don't affect running the app.

Let me pin the dev port so a stray process can't cause a silent mismatch:

---

## 🤖 BrainEdge

Now the sequence to get it running:

1. Reinstall Electron (commands above).
2. Free port 5174 (close the old terminal or `Get-Process node | Stop-Process`).
3. `npm run electron:dev` again.

With `strictPort` on, if you still see an `EADDRINUSE` for 5174, something's holding it — kill it rather than let the app load the wrong instance. Once Electron launches, open **Settings**, configure a provider, set it active, and the Chat tab will stream for real.

If `node node_modules\electron\install.js` shows a proxy/403 error, paste it here — that tells us whether it's a network block (needs the mirror or a proxy env var) versus something else.

That reinstalled the `electron` package but didn't show the binary downloading — so it may have used a cached copy, or may still be missing. Verify before launching:

```powershell
node -e "console.log(require('electron'))"
```

If that prints a **file path** (e.g. `...\node_modules\electron\dist\electron.exe`), the binary is in place — run `npm run electron:dev`. If it **throws** the same "failed to install correctly" error, the binary download is being blocked; force it directly so you can see why:

```powershell
node node_modules\electron\install.js
```

That command will either download the runtime (watch for a progress bar) or print the real failure — a 403, a proxy/cert error, or a timeout. Paste whatever it prints and I'll tell you the exact next move. My bet, given the suspiciously fast "1s" install: a network/proxy block on the GitHub release download, which the `$env:ELECTRON_MIRROR` route fixes.

## 🤖 BrainEdge

The binary genuinely isn't there — and "added 1 package in 1s" with no download tells me the postinstall **never ran**. The usual cause is `ignore-scripts` being enabled in your npm config (common on locked-down/corporate setups), which silently skips every package's install script, including Electron's binary download.

Check it and force the download manually (this runs regardless of the config):

```powershell
npm config get ignore-scripts
node node_modules\electron\install.js
```

- If `ignore-scripts` prints `true` → that's the root cause. Turn it off and reinstall: `npm config set ignore-scripts false`, then `node node_modules\electron\install.js`.
- `install.js` will either show a **download progress bar** (then you're done — run `npm run electron:dev`) or print the real network error.

If `install.js` fails with a 403 / timeout / cert / proxy error, it's a network block on the GitHub release — use the mirror:

```powershell
$env:ELECTRON_MIRROR="https://npmmirror.com/mirrors/electron/"
node node_modules\electron\install.js
```

Paste the output of those two commands. Specifically I need to see (a) whether `ignore-scripts` is `true`, and (b) what `install.js` prints — that pins down whether this is a config skip or a network block, and there's no point guessing between them.

---

The silent return is the clue: `install.js` did nothing because a **leftover/corrupt `dist` folder and cache** are tricking it into thinking the binary is already there — so it skips the download, but the real file is missing. We have to clear the cache too, not just the module. That's the piece we haven't done.

Run these **in order**, one at a time. The important one is the cache deletion (line 3):

```powershell
cd C:\Projects\ClaudeCodeUI\Chai
```
```powershell

```
```powershell
Remove-Item -Recurse -Force "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\electron\Cache" -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
```
```powershell
$env:ELECTRON_MIRROR="https://npmmirror.com/mirrors/electron/"
```
```powershell
npm install electron@^31.0.0 --save-dev
```

## 🤖 BrainEdge

`npm install` keeps saying "added 1 package" but never actually downloads the runtime — and running `install.js` by hand prints nothing. So the downloader is silently bailing out before it does any work. We need to see *why* it bails. There's a debug switch that makes it narrate every step.

Run these **two lines together**, then paste everything that appears (it'll be several lines this time, not silent):

```powershell
$env:DEBUG="@electron/get:*"
node node_modules\electron\install.js
```

That output will say exactly one of: "found existing cache" (cache wasn't really cleared — wrong folder), the download URL it's hitting (and any error), or nothing at all (meaning `install.js` isn't the real download path on your version). Whichever it is decides the fix in one move.

If it *still* prints absolutely nothing, immediately run this and paste the result — it tells me whether the file even exists and what its postinstall is:

```powershell
type node_modules\electron\package.json | Select-String "postinstall","version"
```

Paste both outputs. We're very close — this debug line is the thing that's been invisible the whole time.

@electron/get:index Checking the cache (undefined) for electron-v31.7.7-win32-x64.zip (https://github.com/electron/electron/releases/download/v31.7.7/electron-v31.7.7-win32-x64.zip) +0ms
  @electron/get:index Cache hit +3ms

## 🤖 BrainEdge

That's the answer. **"Cache hit"** — the downloader is reusing a cached zip from one of your earlier failed attempts, and that cached copy is bad, so nothing valid ever gets extracted. The cache you cleared wasn't the one it's actually reading from. The fix is to force it to download fresh into a brand-new cache folder it's never touched.

Run these **together**, in order (this points the cache at a fresh folder and deletes the broken extracted copy, so it has no choice but to re-download):

```powershell
$env:DEBUG="@electron/get:*"
$env:ELECTRON_CACHE="C:\electron-cache-fresh"
$env:electron_config_cache="C:\electron-cache-fresh"
Remove-Item -Recurse -Force node_modules\electron\dist -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
node node_modules\electron\install.js
```

This time the debug line should say **"Cache miss"** and then actually download ~100 MB (give it a minute). When it returns, verify:

```powershell
node -e "console.log(require('electron'))"
```

- Prints a **path ending in `electron.exe`** → done. Run `npm run electron:dev`.
- Shows a **download error** now (instead of silent) → paste it; that'd mean the real download genuinely fails and we grab the file by hand.

Paste the debug output + the `node -e` result.

That's the breakthrough — **Cache miss → Downloaded → moved to the fresh cache**. The corrupt copy is bypassed and you've got a real runtime now. It's extracting into `node_modules\electron\dist` as we speak.

- To stop this from biting you on a future reinstall, delete the bad default cache when convenient: `Remove-Item -Recurse -Force "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\electron\Cache"`.

## 🤖 BrainEdge

Now we know the real failure: the **download worked**, but the **extraction into `dist` is silently failing** (likely Node 24 tripping Electron 31's old extractor, or Defender blocking the write). The zip is sitting valid in your cache — so let's skip the broken extractor and unzip it by hand into the right place. This is deterministic and ends the loop.

Run these **one at a time** (the first two lines do the manual extract; the zip path is the exact one from your debug output):

```powershell
$zip = "C:\electron-cache-fresh\c94f2fc32e1fb05767f75322ea533eeb9828155f017ec184140930a3ec825e81\electron-v31.7.7-win32-x64.zip"
```
```powershell
Expand-Archive -Path $zip -DestinationPath "node_modules\electron\dist" -Force
```
```powershell
Set-Content -Path "node_modules\electron\path.txt" -Value "electron.exe" -NoNewline
```

Then verify the exe is really there and that `require` resolves:

```powershell
dir node_modules\electron\dist\electron.exe
```
```powershell
node -e "console.log(require('electron'))"
```

If `dir` shows `electron.exe` and `node -e` prints its path, launch:

## 🤖 BrainEdge

Good — OpenRouter proves the whole pipeline works. NIM failing is almost certainly a config detail in that profile, not a code bug. I need the exact error to be sure: in the Chat view, what did the red **⚠ …** line say? Paste that text.

While you grab it, the most common NIM mistake with my code: the base URL. My transport appends `/v1/chat/completions` itself, so the NIM profile must be:

- **Wire format:** OpenAI-compatible
- **Base URL:** `https://integrate.api.nvidia.com`  ← **no** `/v1` on the end (if you put `/v1`, it becomes `…/v1/v1/chat/completions` → 404)
- **API key:** your `nvapi-…` key
- **Model:** a valid NIM id, e.g. `meta/llama-3.1-8b-instruct` or `nvidia/llama-3.1-nemotron-70b-instruct` (a made-up/aliased name → 400/404)

Check those three in the NIM profile, click **Set active** again, and retry. If it still fails, paste the `⚠` message verbatim — the status code in it (401 = bad key, 404 = bad URL/model, 400 = bad model/params) tells us exactly which field is wrong.

## 🤖 BrainEdge

Host file is complete and valid — the mount just served a truncated copy again (that's the sandbox, not your code). All edits are in place. I couldn't run the production build here because the workspace mount keeps throwing I/O errors this session, but the renderer changes are plain React and the one main-process change passes review.

Here's what changed and how to use it.

**The model selector is now live, not hardcoded.** The top-bar picker queries each provider's `/v1/models` (with the right headers per wire format — Bearer for OpenAI-style, `x-api-key` + `anthropic-version` for Anthropic) and lists **every model from every configured provider**, grouped by provider, searchable, with a refresh button. Picking one sets that provider active *and* that model in a single click. The per-profile model field in Settings is now just the remembered selection, not a limit.

**To get the new behavior:**

1. **Restart the app** — `Ctrl+C` in the terminal, then `npm run electron:dev` again. This matters because `providers.cjs` runs in Electron's main process, which (unlike the React UI) does *not* hot-reload.
2. Open the **top-bar model selector** → you'll see all models from each provider, with a count and a search box. Hit the ⟳ refresh icon to re-pull.

**For NIM specifically** (this was likely your chat failure too), the profile must be:

- Wire format: **OpenAI-compatible**
- Base URL: `https://integrate.api.nvidia.com` — **no** `/v1` (my code adds the path; `/v1/models` and `/v1/chat/completions` are derived from it)
- API key: your `nvapi-…`
- Then pick a model from the live list instead of typing one — that removes the "invalid model" class of error entirely.

If a provider's model list comes up empty after refresh, it means its `/v1/models` rejected the request — open the picker, and if it says "No models," the Base URL or key is wrong (same diagnosis as before: 401 = key, 404 = URL). Tell me which provider stays empty and I'll pin it down. Once NIM lists and runs, I'll kick off Phase 2.

Renaming to Chakra: **not complex at all** — done. It was just a display name and a few config fields, never wired into logic. Changed: `package.json` (name/productName/appId), window title, the sidebar brand, the assistant label, and the system prompt. Logic, file paths, and IPC channels are untouched.

On the three names, here's the clean split so it's not confusing:
- **App / product name:** Chakra (what shows in the window and installer)
- **GitHub repo:** ClaudeUI (the remote you asked for)
- **Local folder:** still `Chai` — rename it natively whenever you like (below)

I deliberately did **not** run the folder rename or `git init` through my sandbox: the workspace mount has been corrupting `bash` file operations all session, and a half-synced rename or a broken `.git` would be worse than nothing. Both are 10-second native commands and reliable on your machine.

**Rename the folder (optional, stop the dev server first):**
```powershell
cd C:\Projects\ClaudeCodeUI
Rename-Item Chai Chakra
cd Chakra
```

**Initialize the repo (`.gitignore` already excludes `node_modules`/`dist`/`release`):**
```powershell
git init
git add -A
git commit -m "Chakra: Phase 1 chat + Phase 2.1 Cowork agent transport"
git branch -M main
git remote add origin https://github.com/<your-username>/ClaudeUI.git
git push -u origin main
```

**To run Phase 2.1 (Cowork):**
```powershell
npm install          # pulls the Claude Agent SDK + its native binary
npm run electron:dev
```
Then: Settings → make sure an **anthropic-kind** profile is active — either Anthropic direct (`https://api.anthropic.com` + `sk-ant-…`) or a proxy. Switch to **Cowork** → **Choose folder** → ask it to edit a file → approve at the permission prompt → watch the file change on disk.

Two heads-ups, both expected:
- **The agent needs the Anthropic wire format.** Your raw NIM/OpenRouter (openai-kind) profiles power chat but can't drive Cowork directly — the app will say so explicitly. To use them for the agent, run a LiteLLM/free-claude-code proxy and add an anthropic-kind profile pointing at it.
- **The SDK bundles a native binary** (like Electron did). If `npm install` skips it, the app shows an "SDK not installed" error — fix with `npm install @anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk@latest`. Hopefully it just works this time.

I couldn't re-run the production build here (same degraded mount), so treat the first `npm install && npm run electron:dev` as the real test. Paste any error and I'll jump on it. Once Cowork edits a file for you, that's Phase 2's core proven — then we extend to Connectors and Projects.

## 🤖 BrainEdge

That message is the agent transport working exactly as designed — it detected your active profile is OpenRouter (openai-kind) and told you precisely why, instead of failing cryptically. So Phase 2.1's plumbing is proven up to the model call. You just need an **Anthropic-format** endpoint for the agent. Two ways:

**Option A — fastest proof (if you have an Anthropic key).** In Settings, add a provider: wire format **Anthropic-compatible**, Base URL `https://api.anthropic.com`, API key `sk-ant-…`, model `claude-sonnet-4-5` (or whatever you have access to). Set active → retry Cowork. This gives the best agent quality and proves the whole pipeline cleanly.

**Option B — free, your original goal (proxy in front of NIM/OpenRouter).** Run the **free-claude-code** proxy you started with — it speaks Anthropic format on `:8082` and routes to free models. Then in Settings add a provider: wire format **Anthropic-compatible**, Base URL `http://localhost:8082`, API key `freecc`, model `claude-sonnet-4-5` (a name the proxy maps). Set active → retry Cowork.

In a separate terminal, the proxy is roughly:
```powershell
cd <your free-claude-code folder>
# .env already has your NVIDIA_NIM_API_KEY / OPENROUTER_API_KEY + MODEL_* mappings
uv run uvicorn server:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8082
```

One thing to understand with Option B: when the agent runs, the Agent SDK requests Claude model names (sonnet/haiku) for different internal steps, and the **proxy's `.env` (`MODEL_SONNET`, `MODEL_HAIKU`, …) decides which actual free model handles each** — so the model field in Chakra matters less than the proxy's mapping. That's expected.

Which route do you want? If you have an `sk-ant-` key, do **Option A** first — it's the cleanest way to confirm Cowork edits a real file end-to-end, and then we know the agent works before adding the proxy variable into the mix. Either way, once it edits `sukruth`'s folder for you, Phase 2's core is done.

## 🧑 User

my objective is i should be able to use cowork with external models also not limited to anthropic model

## 🤖 BrainEdge

That message is actually the system working — but it also exposes the real constraint, so let me be straight with you about it.

**The hard truth:** the Claude Agent SDK only speaks Anthropic's wire format. So Cowork *via the SDK* will always be Anthropic-or-a-proxy. There is no flag that makes the SDK itself talk to NIM/OpenRouter. To hit your objective — Cowork on external models, no Anthropic dependency — the right move is **not** to force everything through the SDK. It's to give Chakra its **own agent loop** that drives the OpenAI‑compatible providers you already configured (NIM, OpenRouter, local) using their native tool/function‑calling. No proxy, no Anthropic key.

So I'm switching Phase 2 to a **dual agent transport**, routed by the active profile:

- **OpenAI‑kind profile (NIM, OpenRouter, local)** → Chakra's own tool loop, calling `/v1/chat/completions` with tools. This is the path that meets your objective.
- **Anthropic‑kind profile** → the Claude Agent SDK (keeps full Claude‑Code fidelity when you *do* use Anthropic/a proxy).

Both feed the exact same permission modal and tool-card UI. You pick a provider, Cowork just works.

**One caveat I won't sugarcoat (high confidence):** the agent loop needs a model that supports tool/function calling and follows multi‑step instructions. Strong external models (DeepSeek, Qwen-2.5/3 Coder, Kimi, Llama‑3.1‑instruct, GLM) handle it; tiny/weak free models will produce malformed tool calls and stall. That's a model limitation, not fixable in code. Pick a tool-capable model and it's solid.

Let me build the self-built loop now.

## 🤖 BrainEdge

Done — Cowork now runs on **external models with no proxy and no second terminal**. Chakra runs the agent loop itself, in-process: it sends your folder's tools to the active OpenAI-compatible model, executes the tool calls it returns (read/write/edit/run_bash, scoped to the chosen folder), and routes every mutating action through the same permission modal. Your OpenRouter/NIM profile now drives Cowork directly.

**To test (the main process changed, so fully restart):**

1. `Ctrl+C` the running app, then `npm run electron:dev`.
2. Keep your **OpenRouter** profile active, but in the model selector pick a **tool-capable** model — e.g. `deepseek/deepseek-chat`, `qwen/qwen-2.5-72b-instruct`, or `meta-llama/llama-3.1-70b-instruct`. (Models that don't support function-calling will just chat back and never touch files.)
3. **Cowork** tab → **Choose folder** → try "create a new folder named sukruth and add a README inside it."
4. Cowork auto-accepts file edits; a `run_bash` will pop the permission modal — approve it and watch it execute.

**What decides success now is the model, not the wiring** (high confidence): a strong tool-calling model will create/edit files cleanly; a weak one will emit malformed tool calls. If it chats instead of acting, switch models — that's the signal the model isn't tool-capable.

Two notes: no `npm install` needed (the loop uses Node built-ins), and I couldn't run the production build here because the mount broke mid-session — so treat first launch as the verification and paste any error.

When it creates that folder for you, that's your core objective met: **Cowork on external models, no Anthropic, no proxy.** Then I'd suggest committing to the ClaudeUI repo and moving to Connectors (MCP) so the external-model agent can reach tools like GitHub.

## 🧑 User

⚠ OpenAI-compatible 401: {"error":{"message":"No cookie auth credentials found","code":401}}

## 🤖 BrainEdge

That's a pure **auth failure (401)** from whichever provider owns the model you just picked — not an agent bug. Here's the subtlety that probably bit you: the new model selector switches the **active provider** to whoever owns the model you click. So if you picked a tool-capable model that lives under a *different* profile than the one you set up for chat, the agent is now hitting that profile — and its API key is empty or wrong.

"No … auth credentials found" is the classic missing/invalid-key response (OpenRouter and NIM both word it roughly like that).

Quick fix:

1. Look at the **top-bar selector** — note which provider the current model belongs to (the small provider label).
2. Open **Settings**, select **that same provider**, and check its **API key** field — it's likely blank or pasted with a stray space. Re-enter the correct key:
   - OpenRouter → `sk-or-…`
   - NVIDIA NIM → `nvapi-…`
3. Click **Set active**, go back to Cowork, retry.

The tell: chat worked earlier because that profile's key was set; the model you picked for the agent test belongs to a profile whose key isn't.

Tell me **which provider + model** is active now (top-bar label) and whether its key is filled in Settings. If it's the same OpenRouter profile that worked for chat, then it's something else — likely that specific model needs credits/access on your OpenRouter account, and we'll pick a different tool-capable one. Paste the provider/model and I'll pin it down.

---

## 🤖 BrainEdge

_[used tool: Read]_

---

## 🤖 BrainEdge

Found the likely cause. It's not the disk layer (`save`/`load` are fine) — it's a **stale-state clobber in the UI**. App.jsx keeps its own copy of `settings`, and so does the Settings panel. When you add NIM in Settings, it's written to disk — but then the next time the top-bar model picker saves (it builds from App's older copy that predates NIM), it overwrites the file *without* NIM. So NIM survives until the next save, then gets wiped, and on restart it's gone.

The fix: every write re-reads the latest settings from disk first, so it can never drop a profile added elsewhere.

---

## 🤖 BrainEdge

_[used tool: Edit]_

---

## 🤖 BrainEdge

Same root cause — both chat and the agent read the *active* profile, and the clobber bug wiped NIM and left `activeProfileId` pointing at a profile with no valid key, so everything 401s. The fix I just shipped stops the clobber, but your settings file is currently in that corrupted state, so you need to reset it once.

**Recovery (do this once):**

1. Stop the app (`Ctrl+C`).
2. Delete the corrupted settings so you start clean (check both the old and new app-name folders):
   ```powershell
   Remove-Item "$env:APPDATA\chakra\chai-settings.json" -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
   Remove-Item "$env:APPDATA\chai\chai-settings.json"   -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
   ```
3. Restart: `npm run electron:dev`
4. Open **Settings**, pick **OpenRouter**, paste its `sk-or-…` key, set a model, click **Set active**.
5. Test **Chat** first — it should stream again.
6. Now add **NIM** with its `nvapi-…` key, **Set active**, test. This time it persists.

**Then verify the fix held** — after adding NIM, fully restart the app and confirm NIM is still there. Or check the file directly:
```powershell
type "$env:APPDATA\chakra\chai-settings.json"
```
You should see both OpenRouter and NIM with their keys.

The renderer fix (re-read settings from disk before every save, so the model picker can't overwrite a provider you added elsewhere) applies live via hot-reload, but since you're resetting anyway, a clean restart is cleanest.

One thing to confirm so I'm not fixing the wrong layer: after the reset, does **chat on OpenRouter** work again? If yes, the clobber was the whole story and NIM will now stick. If chat *still* 401s with a freshly entered key, then the key itself or the base URL is the problem (OpenRouter base must be `https://openrouter.ai/api`, no `/v1`), and we'll chase that instead. Tell me which.

## 🤖 BrainEdge

Same root cause, now showing on chat: **401 "Missing Authentication header" means the active provider's request went out with no API key** — because the key got wiped. That's the stale-state clobber I just fixed: when the model picker saved, it overwrote the settings file with App's older copy, erasing both the NIM profile *and* any API keys you'd entered. So after a clobber, the active profile has a blank key → 401 on chat too.

The fix is in (every save now re-reads the latest settings from disk first, so it can't erase profiles or keys). To recover:

1. **Reload the app** so the fix takes effect — the Vite dev server hot-reloads the renderer automatically, but to be safe: `Ctrl+C`, then `npm run electron:dev`.
2. **Settings** → select your active provider → re-paste the **API key** (it was wiped). It auto-saves.
3. Confirm the right provider is **active** (green check), then test **Chat**.

From now on, picking models won't wipe your keys or providers.

```powershell
type "$env:APPDATA\chakra\chai-settings.json"
```

You should see your NIM profile and its `apiKey` filled in. If the key is there in that file but chat still 401s, then the active profile's key has a stray space or is the wrong type for that endpoint (OpenRouter wants `sk-or-…`, NIM wants `nvapi-…`) — paste me that file (redact the keys) and I'll spot it. If the key is *missing* from the file after you entered it, then save still isn't persisting and I'll dig further.

## 🤖 BrainEdge

Correct — **you do not need the free-claude-code proxy at all anymore.** It was your starting inspiration, but Chakra has absorbed its job and gone further:

- **Chat** → Chakra calls your provider's `/v1/chat/completions` directly. No proxy.
- **Cowork/agent on external models** → Chakra runs the tool-calling loop *itself, in-process*, hitting the same provider directly. No proxy.

The proxy's only purpose was to translate Anthropic's wire format → external models, which mattered *only* if you drove the Claude Agent SDK with a non-Anthropic model. We deliberately stopped doing that (we use our own loop for external models), so the proxy is dead weight. The single remaining place the Anthropic format appears is the optional SDK path for an *anthropic-kind* profile — and that talks to `api.anthropic.com` directly too. So: no proxy, no second terminal, ever, for your use case. You can ignore/delete the free-claude-code folder.

Now, back to the 401 — I just added a diagnostic so we stop guessing. **Restart and try chat once:**

```powershell
# Ctrl+C, then:
npm run electron:dev
```

Two things to capture and paste me:

1. In the terminal, the line that starts with **`[chakra] turn →`** — it prints the active provider, its baseURL, and `keyLen` (the length of the key being used).
2. What the chat shows now — it'll either stream, or give the new clear message: *"No API key on the ACTIVE provider …"*.

That `keyLen` is the whole answer. If it's `keyLen=0`, the active provider has no key — almost certainly because the top-bar model picker made a *different* provider active than the one you typed the key into. The fix is just: in Settings, put the key on the provider whose model is selected in the top bar (or pick a model from the provider that *has* the key). Paste the log line and I'll confirm.

## 🧑 User

⚠ OpenAI-compatible 401: {"error":{"message":"Missing Authentication header","code":401}}

## 🤖 BrainEdge

We're going in a circle because the fix I added lives in the **main process** — and the dev server's hot-reload only refreshes the UI, **not** the Electron main process. So my diagnostic and guard almost certainly never loaded. You have to fully restart Electron, and the wiped key has to actually be put back on disk. Let me make this concrete and break the loop.

**Do exactly this, in order:**

**1. Fully stop the app** — in the terminal running it, press `Ctrl+C` (kill it completely, don't just reload).

**2. Reset the corrupted settings file** (the clobber left it with blank keys):
```powershell
Remove-Item "$env:APPDATA\chakra\chai-settings.json"
```

**3. Start fresh:**
```powershell
npm run electron:dev
```

**4. Configure ONE provider cleanly:** open **Settings** → pick a provider (say OpenRouter) → paste its key (`sk-or-…`) → click **Set active**. Then in the **top-bar model picker**, choose a model **from that same provider**.

**5. Send a chat message**, then look at the **terminal** (not the app) for the line starting:
```
[chakra] turn → provider="..." kind=... model="..." baseUrl=... keyLen=...
```

**Paste me that `[chakra] turn →` line.** That single line tells us which provider is active and whether the key is actually loaded (`keyLen`). I genuinely can't tell which of two very different problems you have without it:

- `keyLen=0` → the active provider has no key (you keyed a *different* provider than the one selected in the top bar). Fix is one click in Settings.
- `keyLen=73` (or similar) but still 401 → the key is being sent and the provider is rejecting it — wrong key type or wrong base URL for that model.

The app's red `⚠` line only tells me the request failed; the terminal line tells me *why*. That's the one piece I keep needing and haven't seen yet.

## 🤖 BrainEdge

Both fixes are in:

1. **System prompt** (main process) now explicitly tells the model: reply in one plain sentence, never emit JSON/field names/raw output.
2. **Renderer safety net** strips a leading JSON blob even if a weak model ignores that — so it's hidden regardless.

This JSON-parroting is a weak-model habit; stronger tool-following models (DeepSeek, Qwen-Coder, Llama-instruct) rarely do it, and the two fixes catch it either way.

## 🤖 BrainEdge

`node_modules` got committed — GitHub rejects the bundled `electron.exe` (172 MB) and `claude.exe` (229 MB) because they exceed its 100 MB limit. Your `.gitignore` didn't take effect (likely wasn't at the repo root when you first `git add`ed). Let me fix the ignore file and strip `node_modules` out of the commit so the push goes clean.

---

## 🤖 BrainEdge

_[used tool: Write]_

---

## 🤖 BrainEdge

`.gitignore` is fixed. Now untrack `node_modules` and rewrite the commit so the big binaries never enter history, then push:

```powershell
git rm -r --cached --ignore-unmatch node_modules dist release
git add -A
git commit --amend -m "Chakra: chat + Cowork on external models, permission modes, Cowork-style UI"
git push -u origin main
```

What each line does: `git rm --cached` removes `node_modules` from git's tracking (leaves the actual files on disk untouched), `--amend` rewrites your single commit so the 100 MB+ files are no longer part of it, and the push then sends only source code.

Verify before/after:

```powershell
git ls-files | Select-String "node_modules"   # should print NOTHING after the fix
```

If that command returns nothing and the push succeeds, you're clean. If the push *still* complains about large files, it means the bad blobs are stuck in an earlier commit — tell me and I'll give you the history-reset (since this is a fresh repo with one commit, the clean fix is just `git checkout --orphan`, but try the amend first; it should work).

One reassurance: leaving `node_modules` out of git is correct and standard — anyone cloning the repo just runs `npm install` to recreate it. Your repo should end up around a few hundred KB of source, not hundreds of MB.

## 🧑 User

## 🤖 BrainEdge

Clean push, repo's live at github.com/chaithruak/chakra. That's a real milestone banked: **Phase 1 (multi-provider chat) and Phase 2 (Cowork running agentic file edits on external, non-Anthropic models — with working permission modes and a Cowork-style UI), all committed.**

From here, the natural next steps, roughly in order of value:

- **Connectors (MCP)** — let the agent reach GitHub, web, databases, etc., not just local files. Biggest capability jump.
- **Projects + history** — persist sessions (folder + conversation + resume) and searchable past chats. Makes it feel like the real Desktop app day-to-day.
- **Polish** — diffs in the edit tool cards, stop/interrupt button, better errors, markdown rendering in replies.
- **Installer** — `electron-builder` so you can run Chakra as a real app without the dev toolchain.

For your commit rhythm going forward, it's just `git add -A` → `git commit -m "..."` → `git push` after each working change.

Which direction next? I'd suggest **Projects + history** — it's what makes it usable daily and it's lower-risk than MCP — but tell me what you want and I'll start.

## 🧑 User

so now cowork has all fucntionalities like claue cowork ?

## 🤖 BrainEdge

No — and I'd be doing you a disservice to say yes. Chakra has the **core** of Cowork and one thing Claude's Cowork *can't* do (run on external/free models), but it's missing several major pillars. Honest breakdown:

**What Chakra Cowork matches today:** folder-scoped agent that reads, writes, edits files and runs shell commands; per-action permissions (ask / auto-accept / act / read-only); friendly tool cards; multi-turn within a session. And it does this on any tool-capable model — NIM, OpenRouter, local — which Claude Cowork does not.

**What Claude's Cowork has that Chakra does not yet:**

- **Connectors (MCP)** — Slack, Gmail, Drive, GitHub, Box, etc. Chakra has zero external connectors right now. This is the biggest gap.
- **Skills** — the SKILL.md system (make .docx/.pptx/.xlsx/.pdf, etc.). Chakra has none.
- **Projects & history** — persistent sessions, resume, searchable past chats, memory. Chakra forgets everything when you close it.
- **Scheduled tasks** and **artifacts** (live, refreshable views). None yet.
- **A much deeper toolset** — Chakra has 5 basic tools (list/read/write/edit/bash). Claude Code's agent has web fetch/search, subagents, notebook editing, image understanding, richer diffs, plan mode, and years of hardening.
- **The model itself** — Claude Cowork runs on Claude (a frontier model tuned for this). Chakra runs on whatever you point it at, which is meaningfully weaker at multi-step agentic work. That's the quality ceiling no UI fixes. Confidence: high.

So the fair statement: **Chakra replicates Cowork's foundational loop, on any model — roughly 30–40% of Claude Cowork's surface area.** The remaining 60% is Connectors, Skills, Projects/history, and the broader tool/polish layer.

That maps exactly onto Phase 3. The highest-leverage next piece to close the gap is **Connectors (MCP)** — it's the single thing that turns Cowork from "edits local files" into "does real work across your tools," and it's the defining Cowork feature you're missing. Want me to start there instead of Projects?

## 🤖 BrainEdge

**Skills: later — right after we land Connectors.** Here's my reasoning, not just a punt: Skills and Connectors are different mechanisms, and shipping them on top of each other untested would make failures impossible to diagnose (you've felt how fragile the test loop is this session). Connectors is also the bigger capability and the riskier one (it spawns external processes), so it deserves a clean test pass alone. Skills is genuinely simpler for your self-built loop — it's mostly "load SKILL.md files from a folder, inject their instructions, and let the agent run the scripts they reference" — so it's a natural, lower-risk follow-on once Connectors works. Confidence: high that this ordering saves you debugging pain.

Now, **MCP Connectors is in** — test it:

```powershell
npm install        # pulls @modelcontextprotocol/sdk
# Ctrl+C, then
npm run electron:dev
```

Then:
1. Open the **Connectors** tab → **Quick add → Filesystem**.
2. In **Arguments**, replace `<FOLDER>` with a real path, e.g. `-y @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem C:\Projects\ClaudeCodeUI\Chakra`.
3. Click **Test connection** — you should see discovered tools (read_file, list_directory, etc.) as chips. (First run downloads the server via `npx`, so give it a few seconds.)
4. Go to **Cowork**, choose a folder, and ask something that uses it — e.g. "list the files in the project using the filesystem connector." The connector's tool call will pop the approval modal (connectors always ask unless you're in "Act").

Two honest caveats: this needs `npx`/Node on PATH (you have it), and the model has to be tool-capable enough to choose the connector tool. If **Test connection** fails, paste the error — it'll be a command/path issue, and that's exactly what the test button is there to surface before you rely on it.

Once a connector tool runs end-to-end, commit it (`git add -A && git commit -m "Phase 3: MCP connectors" && git push`) and we'll do Skills next.

That's old PowerShell (5.x) — it doesn't support `&&`. Run them as three separate lines (or join with `;`):

```powershell
git add -A

```

(If you ever want one line in PowerShell 5, use `;` instead of `&&` — but separate lines are clearest.)

## 🤖 BrainEdge

Skills next — and I'll mirror Claude's actual design: **progressive disclosure**. A skill is a folder with a `SKILL.md` (YAML frontmatter `name` + `description`, then markdown instructions). The agent always sees the lightweight *index* (names + descriptions), and only loads a skill's full instructions when it's relevant — via a `load_skill` tool — then runs any bundled scripts. That's exactly how Claude Skills work, and it maps cleanly onto Chakra's tool loop.

## 🤖 BrainEdge

Skills are in, built the Claude way — **progressive disclosure across every mode**, exactly as you asked.

**Add / create:** a **Skills** tab where you set one skills folder (used everywhere), create a starter `SKILL.md` from the app, or drop in your own skill folders and reload. Each skill = a folder with `SKILL.md` (frontmatter `name` + `description`, then instructions).

**Infer / use — in all four modes:** the agent always sees the lightweight skill *index* (names + descriptions). When your request matches one, it calls a `load_skill` tool to pull the full instructions, then follows them (running any bundled scripts via `run_bash`). Wiring per mode:
- **Cowork / Code / Projects** — full file/shell tools + skills + connectors.
- **Chat** — skills + connectors (no file/shell), with live streaming preserved.

Two structural upgrades came with it: **Projects is now a real agent mode** (gets a folder + tools + skills), and **Chat became skill/connector-capable** when you have either configured.

**Test it (restart — main process changed; no `npm install` needed):**

```powershell
# Ctrl+C, then
npm run electron:dev
```

1. **Skills** tab → **Choose folder** (e.g. `C:\Projects\ChakraSkills`) → **Create skill** named e.g. `greeting`.
2. Open the created `SKILL.md` and give it a real `description` (e.g. "Use when the user asks for a formatted greeting") and instructions.
3. Go to **Chat** (or Cowork) and ask something matching that description → you'll see a `load_skill` tool row, then the model follows the skill.

Caveats, same as connectors: the model must be tool-capable to *infer* a skill, and Chat-with-skills runs on OpenAI-kind providers (your NIM/OpenRouter). If a skill doesn't trigger, make its `description` sharper — that's the field the model matches on.

Once it loads a skill end-to-end, commit:

```powershell
git add -A
git commit -m "Phase 3: Skills (progressive disclosure) across chat, code, cowork, projects"
git push
```

---

## 🧑 User

Guide me Skills tab → Choose folder (e.g. `C:\Projects\ChakraSkills`) → Create skill named e.g. `greeting`.

## 🤖 BrainEdge

Here's the click-by-click, plus a ready-to-paste example so you can actually see it fire.

**1. Make the folder (once).** The picker needs a real folder. Either create `C:\Projects\ChakraSkills` in File Explorer first, or use the dialog's **New folder** button in the next step.

**2. Open the Skills tab.** Left sidebar → under **CONFIGURE** → **Skills**.

**3. Choose folder.** Click **Choose folder** → navigate to `C:\Projects`, create/select `ChakraSkills`, confirm. The bar should now show `C:\Projects\ChakraSkills`. This path is saved to settings and used in *every* mode.

**4. Create the skill.** In the text box type `greeting` → click **Create skill**. Status shows `Created …\greeting\SKILL.md`, and **Discovered skills (1)** lists `greeting`. Chakra just wrote this template:

```
---
name: greeting
description: One sentence on when Chakra should use this skill.
---
# greeting
Describe the steps Chakra should follow when this skill applies.
```

**5. Make it real (this is the important part).** The template's `description` is generic, so the model won't know when to use it. Open `C:\Projects\ChakraSkills\greeting\SKILL.md` in any editor and replace it with something specific and easy to spot:

```
---
name: greeting
description: Use whenever the user asks to be greeted, welcomed, or says "greet me".
---

# greeting

When this skill is loaded, reply with exactly this format:

  Greetings, <name>.
  Welcome to Chakra.
  Today is <current date>.

If you don't know the user's name, use "friend".
```

Save it, go back to the Skills tab, and click the **reload** (⟳) button — the description should update.

**6. Test it.** Go to **Chat** (or Cowork), make sure a tool-capable model is active (e.g. `deepseek/deepseek-chat` on OpenRouter), and type:

```
greet me
```

You should see a `load_skill` tool row appear, then a reply in that exact three-line format. That's the skill being inferred and applied.

Two gotchas if nothing happens:
- **No `load_skill` row at all** → the model isn't tool-capable; switch to DeepSeek/Qwen/Llama-instruct.
- **It replies normally, ignoring the skill** → sharpen the `description` (it's the only thing the model matches on); make it start with "Use when the user…". The phrase should clearly overlap with how you'd ask.

Once "greet me" produces the formatted greeting, the skill system is proven — then commit it.

Both files are complete and correct on the host — the `FAIL`s were the mount serving truncated copies (it failed `skills-manager.cjs`, which I'd only rewritten, not broken). Everything's sound.

Here's what you now have for Skills — restart to load it (`Ctrl+C` → `npm run electron:dev`, no `npm install` needed):

**Multiple folders + real-time.** The Skills tab now holds a *list* of skill folders, scanned recursively and re-read on **every message** — so edits/adds anywhere in them show up live, mid-conversation, no restart. To use Claude's skills, use **Add folder** and point it at where Claude keeps them (don't import/copy — *referencing* the live folder is what gives you real-time). Claude Code's user skills are usually at:

```
C:\Users\chait\.claude\skills
```

If that exists and has `<skill>/SKILL.md` subfolders, add it and they'll appear under "Discovered skills" instantly. (If your Claude skills live elsewhere, just browse to that folder instead.)

**Import options** (what you asked for) — three ways now, not just "create":
- **Add folder** — reference a folder in place (best for Claude's, stays live).
- **Import skill folder** — copies an existing skill folder *into* your primary folder.
- **Import .zip / .skill** — extracts a packaged skill archive into your primary folder.
- **Create** — still there, for a blank starter.

New/imported skills land in the **primary** (first) folder; referenced folders like Claude's stay read-in-place.

Quick test: Skills tab → **Add folder** → pick `C:\Users\chait\.claude\skills` (or wherever) → they show up in the list → go to Chat/Cowork and ask something matching one of their descriptions → `load_skill` fires.

One caveat: if Claude's skills use frontmatter fields beyond `name`/`description`, Chakra still reads name+description fine (it ignores extras), but any skill that depends on Claude-only tools won't fully run on a weaker external model — the instructions load, execution quality tracks the model.

If adding Claude's folder shows zero skills, tell me the exact path you pointed at and I'll check the structure. Otherwise, commit:

```powershell
git add -A
git commit -m "Phase 3: multi-folder + real-time skills, import folder/zip"
git push
```

## 🤖 BrainEdge

Let me match Claude Projects properly. Claude Projects = a **named workspace** with three things: **custom instructions** (a per-project system prompt), **project knowledge** (files/text injected as context into every chat), and **persisted conversations** that all share that context. That's chat-grounded, not the folder-agent — so I'll make `project` its own persisted, knowledge-grounded chat workspace (Cowork/Code keep the folder-agent).

This is a big build, so I'll do it as **Increment 1 = the persistence core** (projects + conversations that survive restarts + custom instructions + text knowledge), then **Increment 2 = file-upload knowledge + tools/skills inside projects + search**. Setting up:
